data.title: Ingles Markets Is A Robust Candidate For Significant Upside
data.description:
Back in March of this year, one company that I decided to reaffirm as a ‘strong buy’ candidate was grocery chain Ingles Markets, Incorporated (NASDAQ:IMKTA) even though the business had been experiencing some weakness leading up to that point, I found myself attracted by just how cheap shares were and the fact that leverage is substantially lower than the leverage of other similar companies. Since then, the stock has underperformed the broader market, rising by only 1.8% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 4.4%. This can be attributed to the fact that fundamental performance continues to worsen. But honestly, even with that factored in, the stock remains attractively priced. In fact, I would even go so far as to call it a deep-value play. Because of this, I have no choice but to keep the firm rated a ‘strong buy’ for now.

Checking out Ingles Markets

Since my last article on Ingles Markets, investors have only had access to data covering a single additional operating quarter. This would be the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue totaled $1.33 billion. This represents a drop of 2.6% compared to the $1.37 billion the company reported just one year earlier. Looking at the data provided by management, this decline was driven by weakness across three of the four product categories that the company makes available for shareholders to see.

The one bright spot was grocery revenue, which consisted of sales involving grocery products, dairy products, and frozen foods. This inched up from $490.5 million to $492 million. Unfortunately, the perishables category dipped from $349.1 million to $348.1 million, while fuel revenue dropped from $169.7 million to $150.6 million. The drop in fuel is not terribly surprising given how volatile fuel prices can be. The biggest hit for the firm, by far, involved sales of its non-food products. These include alcoholic beverages, tobacco products, pharmacy products, and health, beauty, and cosmetic products. Revenue here plummeted from $305.9 million to $286.2 million.

Management does not provide much in the way of detail when it comes to the drivers behind these changes. But they do provide other interesting data instead. In the image above, you can see a breakdown of their retail grocery sales. This excludes its ‘other’ sales, which involve the company's fluid dairy and shopping center rentals revenue. You can see that a lot of this sales decline can be attributed to certain one-time events. A lot of it, a total of $30.6 million, involves three stores that the company has that were damaged as a result of Hurricane Helene last year. Most of that pain would be from stores that, as of the end of the second quarter of this year, remained closed. The company also saw a $9.7 million hit associated with the change in timing for the Easter holiday. In fact, when you strip out all of these things, you only see an $8.8 million decline in comparable store sales. And seeing as how fuel sales dropped $19.1 million year-over-year, with that decline almost certainly caused by declining fuel prices, none of this is surprising to me. In fact, management said that total grocery comparable store sales, excluding fuel, popped by 0.8% year-over-year.

Even though these were one-time events, Ingles Markets had to deal with pain on the bottom line as well. Net income dropped from $31.9 million last year to $15.1 million this year. Some of this was because, in the second quarter of 2024, the company booked a $7.7 million gain involving assets that it sold off. This ended up being property that was adjacent to some of its locations. By comparison, this year, it saw a loss of $0.2 million from those types of activities. The company also experienced an increase in operating and administrative costs from 20.8% of sales to 21.8%. This was primarily because of higher repairs and maintenance costs of $2.8 million, and professional fees that were also related to the inclement weather last year that hit the company to the tune of $1.1 million.

Other profitability metrics for the firm also worsened. Operating cash flow, for example, fell from $70.5 million to $63 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $53.9 million to $46.7 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $66.4 million down to $52.4 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. This shows a similar weakness. As the image below illustrates, this can be attributed to some of the same issues that impacted the company negatively during the second quarter of this year compared to the same time last year. It is worth noting, however, that the firm did see a pretty big drop in comparable store sales of $153.5 million. That is inclusive of fuel revenue, which did decline by $53.2 million. The fact that there was an improvement in comparable store sales in the second quarter of the year compared to the first quarter of the year does make me feel a bit more optimistic.

As was the case with the second quarter of the year, the first half of the year in its entirety resulted in a decline in net profits for the business. Net income was cut by more than half from $75.3 million to $31.7 million. Some of this was because of weather-related issues, with the total impact there amounting to $8.2 million. However, the company actually enjoyed a reduction of $10.3 million when it came to salaries and wages because of the temporary closure of four stores, three of which remain closed today. So if anything, when it comes to the operating and administrative side of things, the aforementioned hurricane had a positive impact on the bottom line. The bigger problem, in addition to the decline in sales that the company experienced, was the fact that the firm booked an $8.3 million gain from the sale of disposable assets during the first half of last year. That compares to a $3 million gain this year. But again, the decline in revenue was the primary culprit here.

Even if we assume that these troubles will persist, and if we annualize the results that the company has seen so far for the year, we would get net profits for 2025 of $44.4 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $192 million, and EBITDA of $206.9 million. Using these estimates, and also using the historical results for the company for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced.

These are quite low from a valuation standpoint, with the one exception being the price-to-earnings multiple projected for 2025. In the chart above, I then took the 2025 estimates and compared Ingles Markets to five similar firms. What I found was that, on a price-to-earnings basis, two of the five competitors were cheaper than it is. But when it came to the other two profitability metrics, our candidate was cheaper than all but one of the firms.

There are some other ways that we should value the company. In the chart above, you can see the price-to-sales multiple of each of these businesses. Once again, only one of the companies ends up being cheaper than Ingles Markets. And then in the chart below, you can see the same type of analysis using the price-to-book multiple. This one is particularly interesting to me.

Normally, when you see such a drastic difference in the price-to-book multiple, it is because of heightened balance sheet risk. This would be most evident by looking at the net debt of the company in question. So, in the chart below, I then looked at the net leverage ratio for not only Ingles Markets, but also for the five firms that I stacked it up against. And what I found was that, by a pretty massive margin, Ingles Markets has far lower leverage than any of these other enterprises. The fact that shares are cheap and the balance sheet of the company is attractive is incredibly bullish to me.

Takeaway

From the data at my disposal, it seems to me as though the market continues to underestimate Ingles Markets. The company is on solid footing, though it has had a few setbacks. The good news is that most of these setbacks have been caused by one-time events that should eventually subside. Given the totality of the circumstances, I have no choice but to maintain Ingles Markets as a ‘strong buy’.

data.title: Halliburton: High-Potential Stock For The Next Oil Down Cycle, But Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
Halliburton (NYSE:HAL) is a crucial company for the oil and gas sector that’s doing key “behind the scenes” work. This makes them exposed to the economy, meaning that if Trump manages to push oil/gas prices down, tariffs continue, OPEC+ keeps ramping up the production, this could become a very interesting play as well (alongside oil stocks) and a nice way to diversify our oil exposure. One major opportunity I see here - on top of a rebound in oil prices - is Trump's drill-friendly policies, which would imply higher CAPEX from oil and gas producers, meaning higher profits for Halliburton. However, I don't believe this is a buy yet since it probably still has a lot more room to fall, as current prices may not be supported by their current financials.

We see the revenue spread pretty well between Completion/Production and Drilling/Evaluation. As expected, the revenue and profit/free cash flow fluctuate based on the environment. After all, despite not selling a commodity, this company is tied to the performance of commodity companies. This is why, during the Q1 earnings call, they even warned about the impact of tariffs.

About 40% of their revenue comes from North America, while international represents the other ~60%, with Middle East & Asia representing their 2nd largest region. This can change, although it also means there's high exposure to NA, which can be bad if tariffs continue and countries retaliate.

Another thing to consider is that oil companies seem to focus a lot more on consolidation (i.e. mergers and acquisitions such as Exxon's (XOM) Pioneer deal, Chevron (CVX) buying Hess, Occidental + CrownRock (OXY), ConocoPhillips + Marathon Oil (COP), etc.). Although everyone (especially Petrobras (PBR) if you look into it) is investing in exploration/new projects, they seem to prefer the same approach as gold miners - consolidation - which adds nothing to the global supply.

Talking about this, Petrobras and many other institutions also expect the global demand for oil to peak around 2030-35, after which it would slowly and steadily decrease.

At the same time, projects are running out globally, and new production will likely be more expensive. I said before that what we now call cheap oil is limited, and new projects already seem to have higher costs due to various reasons (such as accessibility). Petrobras sees some replacement challenges, meaning that their current projects (operational + currently in development) aren’t enough to keep the production at the current level. This is why they’re expecting to spend ~$2 bil per year on exploration until 2029. And Petrobras is an industry leader in proven reserves-to-production ratio, so the general market will probably have even bigger struggles and higher needs for investments.

Note that Petrobras has been working with Halliburton so far and will likely continue to do it. In January 2025, Halliburton secured its largest service contract with Petrobras to date - a three-year agreement to deliver integrated drilling services on several offshore fields in Brazil - after securing a few other contracts with them last year as well.

They can make a good amount of money for a $16.85 bil market cap. Currently, the TTM Price/FCF ratio = ~7.2, which is actually very interesting and below their 10-year median of ~9.

Currently, that’s around their third-lowest ratio ever (exceptions being 2016 when the FCF was negative and 2020), and today is not even a significant downside. In good (recent) times, we’ve seen it even at 3x the current level.

But, don’t expect them to make this much forever. The past 2-3 years have been exceptional for the company as all oil companies ramped up spending after oil prices rebounded significantly from pandemic lows, peaking above $120 per barrel in mid-2022. That's twice the current level! Plus, CAPEX were much lower in 2020 due to the pandemic/lockdowns/etc. Together, these incentivized companies to invest in new projects. But, at the same time, interest rates are still very high, and CAPEX levels are still below the pre-2015 boom era, as companies focus on capital discipline and cost savings/cutting. So, I’d be careful with this ratio.

Based on their latest earnings report, we see that, financially, they’re in a very good position, with the current assets almost covering the current liabilities + long-term debt. The equity is no longer that far from the market cap, so they seem to be in line with most US oil producers, which makes sense. At current prices, an oil company like Exxon could even buy them if they want to save some costs. This company has quite similar margins (~8% levered FCF) compared to Exxon/Chevron/Shell (7-10%), so it wouldn’t be impossible to see them want to save on the long-term costs while also diversifying their own operations and benefiting from the competition.

The dividend + buybacks represent at least 50% of their annual FCF, of which about 25% (~$600 mil) went into dividends and the rest into buybacks last year. However, they recently ramped up the buybacks, getting to $1 bil TTM, meaning about a 6% buyback yield, on top of the 3.5% dividend. But, remember that if the FCF drops, these will drop as well (bad/normalizing environment). With oil at $60, we are already approaching a period of CAPEX-cutting for many oil companies, so Halliburton might be affected due to the lower spending from their clients.

Overall, I expect similar volatility to oil stocks, meaning they can be a great pick during bad times, as a rebound in the oil industry will come with higher spending, which translates into more profit for Halliburton - as seen in recent years.

Short-term, I expect them to continue to fall if Trump/OPEC+ keep pushing oil prices lower, making it a potential buy well below its average levels. Also, Trump’s “drill, baby, drill” approach could be great for them in the medium/long-term - even with prices falling in the near future - since that could require more services that Halliburton provides.

Another potential opportunity I see in the falling this much is the potential to become an acquisition target. Someone like Exxon/Shell (SHEL)/etc. could easily afford to buy them, potentially cutting some of their operational costs and expanding vertically. This could be especially likely to see from a company like Exxon, who planned to reduce their production costs significantly by 2030.

Valuation:

Note 1: their Price-to-FCF ratio has varied significantly over the past years due to an unusual environment caused by COVID-19 and the high volatility we've seen in commodities. I generally take a more conservative approach when it comes to commodities or related companies, so I will go with ratios below their 10-year median for my estimations.

Note 2: for their FCF assumptions, I will use Halliburton + the industry's historical data and try to adjust it to inflation. Despite it and acquisitions over the past decade, Halliburton's revenue is almost identical to back when we've had similar market conditions, so there shouldn't be that much unpredictability.

Scenario 1

Assuming that oil prices go down to something like $50/bbl (similar to 2016), which would imply issues and lower CAPEX for many oil producers, let’s say they get to $1 bil per year. Say that the ratio is at 10 - which seems like an okay average P/FCF/ ratio during the downcycle for a US oil company (although it can drop a lot more in the short-term if there is panic) - and that’s a $10 bil market cap. So, a price of $11.5 and a ~40% loss. However, a 50% average payout if I buy at such a level could add up to ~8.5% average yields, so the upside from here could be quite interesting, as we’ll see in Scenario 3.

And this is actually around their equity, so falling below this could even lead to them being the target of an acquisition.

Note: $50 is definitely not the bottom of oil - especially in the short term - so things can get much worse, of course. Their financial health allows them to survive for a while, however, but it wouldn't be pleasant for the stock given the likely negative reaction from the market.

Scenario 2

With oil at $65/bbl, I think it’s reasonable to assume that they can make around $1.7 bil on average, which is in line with the level from 2018 + inflation-adjustments when oil was at around $65. Most oil companies on the planet that I’ve seen should be comfortable at this level to invest enough and not need to cut the CAPEX at all. Say the ratio is at 12, meaning a market cap of $20.7 bil and a price of $23.82. This is around 20% higher than the current price, so it seems justified since oil is also lower than in this scenario.

Again, the P/FCF ratio is low now, but the environment has been favorable for them over the past couple of years, so their FCF might drop soon, depending on politics as well.

Scenario 3

With oil maintaining at around $80/bbl, they could probably make ~$2.5 bil since everyone would ramp up investments/production/exploration (similar to 2023 + inflation). Put the ratio at 15, since the market would probably love oil stocks at that level (even if this is still below their median), meaning a $37.5 bil market cap. That’s a price of $43.15, which is very similar to the peak we’ve seen in the past half a decade and a profit of around 120% from the current level.

As I said in Scenario 1, a 50% payout here is a ~12.5% yield for that price, plus potential for special payouts.

My entry target + conclusions:

So, Halliburton is a healthy and profitable company that’s vital for the industry. They’re one of the backbones of oil and gas, which I believe still has at least a decade of normal cyclicality in it (especially thanks to Trump and his oil-and-gas-friendly policies now) before seeing signs of a decline. In the meantime, they’ll have to look into ways of adapting to newer energy sources, but that’s probably a worry for the next 1-2 CEOs, not now.

Since I think there are better companies to buy if oil only dips instead of crashing (e.g. Petrobras (PBR)), I think I’d be as greedy as possible with this one. As such, my entry target would be something around $13-15 - as close to Scenario 1 as possible - to maximize the potential returns and margin of safety.

My rating: On the watch list. Not a buy for me yet.

data.title: Tesla: Robotaxi Is Set To Unlock New Quantum Leap
data.description:
Introduction

My March recommendation to buy the Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) dip was quite good and timely because it was a solid rebound from the $225 level and the share price is currently 47% higher. The good part is that the rebound is unlikely to be over because TSLA's share price is still below its fair value. Fundamentals are all good, with the big robotaxi catalyst expected over the next couple of weeks.

The competition in EV intensifies, but Tesla is also aggressively working on diversifying its business mix, which makes the company more resilient. Since there is less upside left on the table compared to March, I slightly downgrade TSLA. Therefore, it is currently just a "Buy."

Fundamental Analysis

Despite intensifying competition in the EV space, Tesla's profitability remains strong. According to Quant Factor Grades, profitability remains this stock's core advantage. Moreover, I want to emphasize the stability of Tesla's A+ profitability, as the grade was the same three and six months ago.

The company continues working on driving down cost per vehicle, which will pay off over the long term once deliveries numbers return to a sustainable growth path. Decreased cost per vehicle will help Tesla to fortify its operating leverage, which will be crucial when the monetary environment becomes more favorable to drive revenue growth.

In addition, despite the intense competition that weighs on average selling prices, Tesla's free cash flow generating capacity remains robust. The TTM FCF margin is positive ($3.3 billion) which helps the company to support its fortress balance sheet. Having such a robust financial position is a great asset while Tesla aggressively ramps up its innovation spending.

In addition, the management works really hard to improve Tesla's business mix. This will protect the company from competition risks in EV. The expected mid-June launch of Tesla's robotaxi offering is likely to be an inflection point. I am confident in Tesla's promising prospects in this domain because the company did a tremendous job from a technological perspective as its full self-driving, or FSD, accumulated billions of miles of supervised driving.

Of course, Alphabet’s (GOOGL) Waymo is ahead of Tesla, as it is already operating in several large U.S. cities. However, Tesla also has robust strategic advantages in the pocket. First of all, the company will operate its own cars, which is likely to be more efficient operationally and financially. In addition, the technology is different, which is another chance for Tesla to differentiate. Waymo relies heavily on high-definition maps and lidar sensors. Tesla relies on a vision-based system powered by AI and vast amounts of real-world data collected from these billions of miles of supervised FSD driving. I am not an expert in autonomous driving technology, but a more data-driven and AI-powered approach looks like a solid differentiator for Tesla.

Last but not least for the robotaxi topic. Even if Tesla struggles to beat Waymo and remains number two in the industry, it will still create substantial value for shareholders as the industry is expected to demonstrate explosive growth. We see that the robotaxi market is expected to reach $188 billion by 2034. My calculations suggest that capturing even a 20% market share will mean that the fair value of Tesla's robotaxi business will be around $415 billion in 2034.

Besides vast potential to capitalize on the potentially explosive robotaxi market, there are other TSLA businesses that gain momentum. The company's Energy business is demonstrating impressive revenue and gross profit dynamic. This has strong potential to create more value for the company as its gross profit is growing exponentially, meaning strong operating leverage. Powerwall deployments are also jaw-dropping, which means that the demand is really hot.

To finalize fundamentals, Tesla's business mix is rapidly evolving, which is crucial to make the company more resilient given intensifying competition in EV. The balance sheet is clean and positions Tesla well to invest heavily in bold and ambitious new projects. Therefore, I remain bullish.

Valuation Analysis

The company's forward P/E ratio setup remains very bullish. Despite the FY2024 actual P/E of 137, it is sound because it is explained by the projected rapid EPS growth forecast from consensus. Thus, the P/E ratio is poised to decline by around three times over the next five years. Given the company's aggressively improving business mix, this forecast looks reasonable. On the other hand, we have to admit that the current forward P/E trajectory is not that attractive as it was in March when the current P/E was below 100.

The DCF model also demonstrated that there is less upside left on the table than it was in March. Due to the stability in interest rates, the WACC remains the same at 7.4%. Despite significantly improving the business mix, the previously used 5.5% constant growth rate was already quite high. Therefore, I also reiterate the same constant growth rate to calculate Tesla's terminal value ("TV"). I do not consider this constant growth rate too high because there are Apple and Microsoft, the companies that maintain double-digit revenue CAGR for decades.

Revenue assumptions for FY 2026-2027 were obtained from consensus. My confidence in the reliability of these assumptions is supported by the fact that FY2026-2027 consensus revenue forecasts were formed from opinions of 20+ reputable Wall Street analysts. This makes the sample representative and reliable. For the years after FY2027, I incorporated a 25-basis-point yearly growth rate decline.

Tesla's historical average FCF margin is 5.48%. This was used as the base-year assumption. Forecasting a robust yearly FCF margin growth looks reasonable considering the company's exposure to several potentially explosive industries and the management's commitment to deliver operating leverage. Thus, I applied a 250-basis-point yearly FCF expansion. Seeking Alpha suggests that there are 3.22 billion outstanding shares of Tesla.

My fair share price estimate for TSLA is $473. The upside potential is 42%, which does not look too high since the stock already demonstrated even higher levels in December 2024.

On the other hand, it is crucial to keep in mind that any DCF model is extremely sensitive to changes in growth assumptions. This especially relates to the constant growth rate, as a slightly lower one (5%) significantly undermines the fair value and only 14% upside potential remains left on the table.

Mitigating Factors

History teaches us that Elon Musk is not only the visionary leader of Tesla, but also frequently is a threat to the share price. I am not an expert in politics, but I think that his involvement in this field was awkward, softly speaking. There were some really controversial moves from him during his not very long political career, which significantly undermined Tesla's image, especially in Europe. The fact that he recently announced that he is leaving DOGE gave some optimism, which already vanished after he made another quite politically loud tweet recently.

The competition in the EV industry continues to intensify. The biggest rival of Tesla, Chinese company BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF), is firing all cylinders. While Tesla experiences struggles overseas due to some of Elon Musk's political statements about Europe, BYD is quite aggressive in capitalizing on this strategic slip from Tesla. This company recently outsold Tesla in Europe for the first time ever. China is a threat for Tesla not only because of BYD, as there are several other players. Players like Li Auto (LI), XPeng (XPEV), or ZEEKR (ZK) are smaller but also quite aggressive and thrive to gain domestic market share at Tesla's expense.

Tesla's earnings surprise record is really weak, which is a red flag, to be honest. There were only five positive EPS surprises over the last twelve quarters, which is a poor performance. Elon Musk has been extremely good at "selling" new groundbreaking projects during recent earnings call, which outshined poor earnings surprise records. Nevertheless, while the market has been ignoring this adverse factor over the last several quarters, it certainly won't last forever.

Conclusion

The stock saw a sharp rally over a relatively short period, and there is not that much upside left on the table. I remain bullish due to Tesla's stellar fundamentals, but I am not buying TSLA as aggressively as I did during the Spring share price weakness. Therefore, I downgraded TSLA from "Strong buy" to just "Buy."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: British American Tobacco: Positive Trading Update, Upgraded Outlook
data.description:
When I last wrote about British American Tobacco p.l.c. (NYSE:BTI) (OTCPK:BTAFF) or BAT in April, its valuations indicated ~12% price upside for 2025. A bigger increase was possible at the present time of uncertainty. Consumer staples were a good defensive sector to buy into and BTI has the additional allure of rich dividends at a time when there was a question mark on potential capital gains.

Investor sentiment-related price upside, however, is difficult to quantify. So, it was a good idea to circle back to BTI once it touched the determined upside from its valuations. That's happened now. Additionally, the company also released its trading update for the first half of this year (H1 2025) earlier this week, which provides more insight into how the year is unfolding for BAT. All of this is discussed below.

Revenue outlook upgraded…

BAT might be many things, but fast-growing, it isn't. So when the company upgrades its revenue outlook at a time of waning demand for tobacco, it's time to pay attention.

The company marginally raised its revenue guidance for the full year 2025 to 1-2% from the earlier 1%. This doesn't sound like very much at all, but the fact that over the past five years, BAT's revenues have basically remained unchanged puts it in perspective.

BAT highlights "strong global growth" for Velo, and its nicotine pouches in the trading update. The product now has ~30% volume market share in the company's key markets. This adds to BAT's value leadership position in vaping, with its brand Vuse.

…supported by the US market

From a geographical perspective, the company highlights improved performance in the US market. This is a significant development for two reasons. One, the market is key to BAT's financial health as it brought in ~44% of the revenues last year, along with ~54% of the adjusted organic profit.

Two, with a weak performance in 2024, the US market increased the risk of affecting BAT going forward. It saw a 3.4% year-on-year (YoY) decline in organic revenue, primarily due to a contraction in its combustibles sales (see Table 1 below), along with a 3.5% contraction in adjusted organic operating profit (see Table 2 below).

BAT expects 2025 to be a better year for the market, though. It forecasts the US to see growth in both revenues and profits in H1 2025 as well as in the full year 2025. This is anticipated to be on account of, one, an improvement in combustibles and two, a positive launch of VELO+, which differentiates from the standard Velo as it has a higher moisture content and is made from synthetically derived nicotine.

Earnings estimates for 2025 and 2026

Unlike its revenue forecasts, the company has maintained its outlook for adjusted operating profit at constant prices, which it expects to see an increase of 1.5-2.5%. To estimate the operating profit, here it's assumed that the number will grow at the midpoint of the guidance range, at 2%. Next, it's assumed that the adjusted net income to operating income ratio will stay constant this year, at 68.3%, the same as last year. This yields an adjusted net income at constant prices of GBP 8.7 billion (USD 11.8 billion).

In its guidance for 2026, the company expects more robust growth. It forecasts a 3-5% revenue increase and a 4-6% rise in operating profit. If the operating profit grows at the midpoint of the guidance range and the ratio of adjusted net profit to operating profit ratio remains constant from 2024 once again, the net income figure will be at GBP 9.1 billion (USD 12.3 billion) next year.

Market valuations compared with peers

With these estimates, the forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio comes in at 8.52x for 2025 and 8.15x for 2026. These are lower than the 8.8x figure the last I checked. But that doesn't make them attractive compared to the stock's five-year average of 8.25x, which essentially indicates that BTI is fairly priced for the next year and a half.

Normally, I'd place a heavy focus on the five-year average. But these aren't normal times. The stock markets have been iffy for much of 2025 so far, and it remains to be seen whether the gains made in the past month can be sustained. In this scenario, defensives like tobacco stocks can still continue to be attractive to investors.

It follows then, that a comparison with peers can provide greater perspective on what's next for BTI. As it turns out, by this analysis, the stock is still attractively priced. Here, I've compared BTI with Altria Group, Inc. (MO), Imperial Brands PLC (OTCQX:IMBBY), and Japan Tobacco Inc. (OTCPK:JAPAY), while leaving out Philip Morris International Inc. (PM).

PM is disregarded because it routinely trades at a much higher market valuation. For example, its current level is 24.4x, which can be explained by the fact that it has made much bigger inroads into next-generation tobacco products compared with the rest of the tobacco set.

The forward P/Es for MO, IMBBY and JAPAY are at 11.22x, 9.14x and 17.31x respectively, for 2025. On average, they reflect a significant upside of 47% for BTI this year alone. Further, for 2026, their respective P/Es are at 10.73x, 8.36x and 11.69x, indicating another 43% potential increase for BTI.

Superior dividend yield

Additionally, BTI's dividend yield continues to look good too. Even though the forward dividend yield at 6.47% is lower than the 7.6% the last I checked, it remains competitive compared with peers. Only Altria has a slightly higher yield at 6.76%, while Imperial Brands is actually at 5.35% and Japan Tobacco is at 4.18%. Philip Morris has the lowest yield of 2.96%.

What next?

BAT then continues to look like a good buy, with a host of arguments stacked in its favour. At the macro-level consumer staples are safer buys at the present time, when the US economy at least, could see a slowdown. The stock's dividend yield might have dropped in the past months, but it still remains competitive compared with peers. As do its market multiples.

The company's latest trading update also shows that the stock continues to be supported by solid fundamentals. The upgrade to its revenues, and in particular further indication of growth in the new categories market are encouraging for BAT. I'm retaining a Buy on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Introducing The Global X Bitcoin Covered Call ETF
data.description:
On June 4th, 2025, we listed the Global X Bitcoin Covered Call ETF (BCCC) on the Cboe BZX Exchange. BCCC is the latest addition to Global X’s covered call and growth ETF suite, and it is designed to offer investors the opportunity to indirectly participate in the price performance of bitcoin ETPs (exchange-traded products), while seeking to harvest option premiums and manage volatility by selling call options on the same underlying security.

Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) intrigue as an investment opportunity stems from a variety of factors, but whether investors choose to lean into its status as a decentralized currency, or simply view it as a speculative potential growth opportunity, the trajectory it has assumed over the last few years has been difficult to ignore. These days, new innovations in exchanges and products have made investing in bitcoin easier than ever. However, should investors wish to take positions, it’s likely they would still need to deal with the levels of volatility it has historically demonstrated. With wide price swings representing a potential deterrent, gaining indirect exposure to the price returns of bitcoin ETPs that provide exposure to bitcoin, while employing a call option writing strategy to exchange a portion of upside price appreciation potential for a stream of premiums, may represent an interesting value proposition.

BCCC Offers Indirect Exposure to Bitcoin Within the Wrapper of an ETF

Investors have sought various means by which to gain exposure to bitcoin over the years. From mining bitcoin itself, to trading it on crypto exchanges and engaging with bitcoin trusts, access has become increasingly seamless and potentially more efficient. Benefits of the progression have included wider accessibility and liquidity. Most recently, the approval of spot bitcoin ETFs and ETFs that trade bitcoin futures have ushered in a new means by which to achieve this exposure.

The Global X Bitcoin Covered Call ETF seeks to achieve indirect exposure to the price returns of bitcoin by buying and selling options on bitcoin ETPs. Specifically, it utilizes long call options and short put options to obtain synthetic exposure to the price returns of these products.

For illustrative purposes only. Illustration assumes the options positions are made at-the-money and does not include the effect of the premiums paid or received for establishing the option positions. BCCC’s options positions may not always be made at-the-money, which could affect the correlation of the returns.

Gaining exposure to bitcoin ETPs in this fashion is potentially less expensive than buying the ETP outright, as options often cost less to purchase than the underlying vehicles they reference. It may also result in fund returns that deviate modestly from that of the underlying ETPs amid a weekly roll period. That said, upon the outcome period’s conclusion, returns are expected to converge to an approximate 1:1 rate.

The Fund Seeks to Provide Weekly Distributions, Utilizing a Systematically Active Approach

Although the Global X Bitcoin Covered Call ETF is an active investment strategy, a series of functions within the fund are aimed at remaining relatively consistent. For instance, portfolio managers will seek to write weekly call options that are at-the-money or slightly out-of-the-money, from which approximately 75% of the call premiums received are expected to be ultimately distributed to shareholders. Additionally, only a portion of the funds’ notional holdings are expected to be covered, on a roll-to-roll basis, which is designed to allow for upside price appreciation potential to be consistently pursued.

One of the important levers that BCCC engages to promote potentially higher premium values is weekly option writing. By writing options week after week, in succession, the fund may be able to generate larger premiums than it would by writing options with longer tenors (time until expiration). Options naturally lose value as they approach their expiration date, and this decay happens more rapidly during the final days before expiration. By repeatedly selling short-term weekly options, rather than longer-term options, the fund can potentially capture this accelerated value decrease more efficiently, allowing it to collect more premiums over time when compared to a strategy using longer-dated options.

In conjunction with the writing of weekly options, the Global X Bitcoin Covered Call ETF also aims to provide investors with a stream of weekly distributions. Each week, when call options are written, approximately 75% of the premium that is received from writing the call option is expected to be distributed to shareholders, while the balance of the premium is reinvested back into the fund. A distribution will offer investors the opportunity to pursue reinvestment or utilize the proceeds elsewhere. Meanwhile, any automatically re-invested premiums ought to provide a measure of support to the fund’s net asset value (NAV).

BCCC May Be Considered a Hedged Bitcoin Strategy

The Global X Bitcoin Covered Call ETF writes call options on bitcoin ETPs that are either at-the-money or slightly out-of-the-money. For the portion of the portfolio on which call options are written, its upside is theoretically capped at the strike level of the written call options. An important consideration, however, is that by leaving a portion of the fund’s notional holdings uncovered, investors may still be able to participate in some of the upside of these bitcoin-tracking products. On the other side of the coin, BCCC will remain fully exposed to the downside price movements of the underlying ETPs. This downside is expected to be partially offset by the premiums that are pursued from writing the options. Operating in this fashion, the fund might make a bitcoin investment more palatable for those that still believe it can produce outsized returns but are apprehensive owing to its historically elevated levels of volatility.

Conveniently, the same measure of volatility that might promote disinterest in a bitcoin investment is something that may well allow BCCC to procure better option premiums and provide more generous distributions. Bitcoin has shown the propensity to operate under these wider levels of volatility, as illustrated by the standard deviation of its daily returns over time relative to some major domestic equity indices.

Conclusion: BCCC May Strike a Balance Between Growth and Distributions

Based on the intermittent periods of remarkable price appreciation that bitcoin has been able to realize over the years, it’s no mystery why it has garnered interest from a broad segment of the investment community. Nonetheless, the peaks and valleys that have been associated with these advances may make an unhedged position in bitcoin feel like a roller coaster ride. By writing call options on a portion of bitcoin holdings, investors have the opportunity to maintain a degree of upside price appreciation potential associated with bitcoin, while managing its volatility through the pursuit of option premiums and distributions. The Global X Bitcoin Covered Call ETF provides that potential exposure and may help accomplish that goal.

Glossary

Long Call Option: A long call option is a contract that gives the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to purchase an asset at a specified price in a certain timeframe by paying a premium.

Short Put Option: A short put option is a contract that obligates the seller to buy an asset at a certain price if the buyer chooses the exercise the option. The seller receives a premium for this obligation.

Strike Price: An option’s strike price is the predetermined price at which the option becomes exercisable by the purchaser.

At-the-Money: An option contract is deemed at-the-money when the price of the underlying instrument is equal to the strike price of the option.

Out-of-the-Money: An option contract is deemed out-of-the-money when the price of the underlying instrument is below that of the strike price of a call option or above that of the strike price of a put option.

Time Value: The portion of an option premium that is based upon the amount of time that remains until the option reaches its expiration date.

Standard Deviation: Standard deviation is a statistical measure of the variation or dispersion within a set of numbers. In investing it is used to discuss the volatility in returns.

Carefully consider the Funds' investment objectives, risk factors, charges and expenses before investing. This and additional information can be found in the Funds' full or summary prospectus, which may be obtained by visiting globalxetfs.com. Read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Original Post

data.title: Europe's AI U-Turn Raises Doubts, Hopes
data.description:
As the EU mobilizes a €200 billion investment designed to spur new AI datacenters and supercomputers, experts warn that it will take more than funding for Europe to catch up in the global AI race.

In April, the European Commission outlined the details of its AI Continent Action Plan, which aims to make the EU a "global leader in AI." The plan calls for the construction of AI factories and gigafactories, aiming to triple the EU's datacenter capacity in the next five to seven years. Under the plan, the EU wants to increase access to high-quality data for training AI models.

Coupled with deregulatory efforts, this strategy marks a significant shift for a region traditionally skeptical of technology companies and inclined toward regulation. Given that long history, experts say the EU's AI efforts will require more than just spending. More profound reforms — including immigration policies and power generation — may be required to enhance Europe's competitiveness.

"There is an entire civil service structure that requires reform, and it needs to be empowered by innovation," said Alexandru Voica, head of corporate affairs and policy at Synthesia Ltd., a leading European AI startup. "You need to send signals from the top and encourage people to experiment with new technologies."

AI gigafactories

In building the AI infrastructure, the EU aims to mirror the success of past government initiatives such as the European Organization for Nuclear Research, an intergovernmental organization operating the largest particle physics laboratory in the world.

"This unique public-private partnership, akin to a CERN [European Organization for Nuclear Research] for AI, will enable all our scientists and companies - not just the biggest - to develop the most advanced very large models needed to make Europe an AI continent,” European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said in the press release announcing the €200 billion investment. The EU will allocate €20 billion, with the remainder coming from private investors.

The EU plans to establish five "AI gigafactories," each anticipated to house about 100,000 state-of-the-art AI chips like those from Nvidia Corp. This is four times more than the current seven AI factories that are being built in countries including Italy, Germany and Spain. All told, the EU will have 13 supercomputers for AI companies and computer scientists to use.

Thomas Regnier, European Commission spokesperson for tech sovereignty, defence, space and research, told S&P Global Market Intelligence that access to the AI infrastructure will be free-of-charge for startups that use it for "innovation purposes." Commercial access will be allowed on a pay-per-use basis.

"AI Factories will bring together the key ingredients that are needed for success in AI: computing power, data and talent," Regnier said.

This time is different

Unlike nuclear research, AI development is moving at breakneck speed. This could present a challenge for European consortia that include collaborative groups formed by EU member states, research institutions and technology hubs.

"Consortia are very good at getting money, but not very good at achieving results," Bert Hubert, the founder of network solutions company PowerDNS.COM BV and a former regulator in the Netherlands, said in an interview with Market Intelligence.

For every European Organization for Nuclear Research success story, there is a Gaia-X. Gaia-X is an initiative to create a "cloud ecosystem," believed to be the European answer to US hyperscalers, Microsoft Corp.'s Azure and Alphabet Inc.'s Google Cloud Platform.

Gaia-X has failed to deliver meaningful results so far. Experts still struggle to understand what the initiative does exactly. The organization repeatedly denied that it is a European hyperscaler, claiming instead that it aims to create a data-sharing environment. Hubert has called for the Gaia-X project to be abandoned, and he is not alone.

"Gaia-X has been an unmitigated disaster," said Johan Vermij, an analyst at Market Intelligence 451 Research.

The fear now is that history will repeat itself with the AI initiative. AI technology is developing much faster than previous technology shifts like mobile and software as a service. And US cloud hyperscalers have a software advantage that is not easy to replicate. On the public cloud, developers can gain quick access to all the chips and software necessary to build AI applications.

Synthesia, a GenAI video generator for enterprise use cases that recently closed a $180 million funding round at a post-money valuation of $2.1 billion, is building its applications on Amazon.com Inc.'s (AMZN) Amazon Web Services and Google Cloud Platform.

"The challenge you have with [EU] supercomputers is that they have specific software stacks. If you build commercial applications in the hyperscalers' cloud, it's much easier to move software workloads, while supercomputers get bottlenecked," Voica said.

The gigafactories can be more useful in solving deep research problems, such as drug discovery, which are not subject to commercial competition. Mistral AI SAS, the leading European provider of foundation models, built its early foundation model, Mistral 7B, on Leonardo, an EU supercomputer in Italy. Mistral complained about the lack of computing power in Europe. It is now reportedly building its own cloud infrastructure. Mistral did not reply to a request for comment.

Synthesia runs its workloads on hyperscalers' datacenters in Europe, but sometimes it needs to tap into the US datacenters due to a lack of capacity on the continent.

European GenAI startups

Market Intelligence counted more than 130 GenAI startups based in Europe. About a third of those are in the UK, and another third in France and Germany combined. Together, these companies have raised a total of more than $5 billion in venture capital since inception, according to Market Intelligence. "There are a couple of companies that are promising, but without government backing, they cannot compete with the US companies," Vermij said.

The EU numbers pale in comparison to the US, which has three times as many GenAI startups that have collectively raised $161 billion. Large multibillion-dollar funding rounds in the US have been closed by leading GenAI foundation model makers such as OpenAI LLC, Anthropic PBC and X.AI Corp.

Changed approach

Experts say the EU would need to make reforms that have little to do with technology itself to boost the GenAI startup scene. Specifically, the region needs more talent and datacenters.

To attract talent, startups say they need a better visa regime. An article by European computer scientists Nuria Oliver, Bernhard Schölkopf and Yann LeCun criticized the EU's approach as project-based rather than person-centered.

"Europe's most successful scientific organizations and programs … believe that the key to groundbreaking innovations lies in identifying and attracting the brightest minds," the trio wrote. "But in the EU, this principle is in danger."

To build more datacenters, a key constraint is the energy power and regulatory requirements. The Netherlands, for instance, imposed a moratorium on new datacenter construction due to a lack of energy. Germany imposed various restrictions on new and operating datacenters as part of the country's ambitious decarbonization goals.

"The EU needs to create the underlying infrastructure, such as the energy grid that allows datacenters to exist and scale," Synthesia's Voica said.

According to 451 Research's Datacenter KnowledgeBase, the European leased datacenter footprint is half that of the US and China.

Data demand

AI companies are also complaining about the lack of European data to train their models. According to Voica, this is where the EU could establish a modern and simplified regulatory framework that would encourage content creators to share data with AI companies without fearing their data will be misused.

Synthesia had to create its own training data by setting up a studio and hiring actors to talk on camera, largely because it was hard to source third-party data. The company recently signed a contract with US-based video and photo library Shutterstock, under which it will only pay for content it chooses to train on.

The EU was the first major jurisdiction to implement regulation of AI technologies under the EU AI Act. The European Commission is now looking to simplify the rules, with AI policy unit chief Kilian Gross recently expressing openness to "targeted changes," Politico reported.

Too much regulation often has unintended consequences. Instead of leveling the playing field between large corporations and small startups, it can advantage deeper-pocketed Big Tech companies who have the resources to hire compliance officers and to fight in courts, if necessary.

"Success will rely heavily on whether the EU can sustain its current momentum politically, especially when translating ambitious plans into concrete actions, particularly through clear policy signals and strategic communication that can attract and retain investment," Öykü Özfırat, a senior policy analyst at Access Partnership, told Market Intelligence.

France taking lead

Separate governments in the EU are doing more. France, once a skeptic of datacenter construction, has now taken the lead in Europe. According to 451's Datacenter KnowledgeBaseGrowth, datacenter capacity growth in France is expected to rise 16% in 2025, the second-highest rate of growth after Ireland.

Mistral is building a massive 1.4 GW power datacenter cluster in Paris with the help of the French government and the United Arab Emirates' sovereign wealth fund MGX, in partnership with Nvidia (NVDA). It follows the footsteps of its American competitor OpenAI, which is building its 1.2 GW cluster in Texas to reduce reliance on its hyperscaler partner Microsoft.

"Given the high-energy demands of datacenters, France is well positioned to take the lead thanks to its extensive nuclear power infrastructure, which can provide a carbon-free energy source for AI datacenters," Vermij said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Petrobras' Recent Weakness Means Opportunity
data.description:
Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) is one of the largest oil companies in the world with a $75 billion valuation. With weak oil prices, the company has underperformed in the market since our last article. The company, as the state oil company of Brazil, has a strong integrated portfolio, but has had consistent governance concerns. Despite that, Petrobras is a valuable long-term investment opportunity at its current price.

Petrobras Operational Results

Petrobras has managed to increase production to almost 2.8 million barrels / day, mid-single-digit growth over the prior quarter.

The company has continued to start up new assets with Búzios, and the company's new FPSO (#7) will produce up to 225 thousand barrels / day and process a massive 12 million cubic meters of natural gas per day. The company has had a new FPSO arrive, and it's continuing to find new discoveries that will support future production.

Petrobras Financial Results

Financially, the net result for this company has been strong performance versus its $70 billion market cap.

The company managed to earn a massive $4.5 billion in FCF for the quarter, up 20% QoQ, with $10.7 billion in EBITDA. The company has announced more than $2 USD in declared dividends, a strong double-digit dividend yield that shows what the company can comfortably afford along with its continued commitments to shareholders.

The company's adjusted EBITDA declined YoY, but the company was impacted by average Brent prices declining from $83 / barrel to $75 / barrel.

Petrobras Investments

The company is continuing to invest dramatically in growth.

The company invested $1 billion in FPSO in the quarter, with FPSOs physical progress improving by 25%. The company has a number of FPSOs under construction, which has led to increased well completion and connections. These investments are key to multi-decade reliable low-cost production from the company's unconventional assets.

The company is continuing to invest dramatically with $16 billion in annual capex. That's more than 20% of the company's market capitalization, which shows how heavily the company is investing versus its valuation. That will enable the company to continue growing its production while reducing costs, which will improve FCF.

The company's ability to drive FCF after these investments is key to future returns.

Petrobras Shareholder Returns

The company's overall debt has hit a very manageable level, which enables it to focus on shareholder returns.

The company's average debt maturity has remained manageable at 12.2 years, despite the high interest rate environment that we're in. At the same time, the company has continued to improve its risk profile with its spread to the US treasury rate now sitting at a mere 2.6%. That is in a higher interest rate environment, but it's still a strong improvement.

The company's financial obligations are primarily leasing with the company's net debt at $56 billion and financial debt at a mere net of $15.3 billion. That's a very manageable level to the company's market cap.

The company remains committed to its strong dividends. The company's announced dividends of $0.16 are $0.32 / ADR share or a ~3% yield. The company plans to pay these dividends in late August and September showing its continued commitment to shareholder returns based on its 45% FCF remuneration policy.

As long as the company's share price remains low it's sitting at a double-digit dividend yield, highlighting its financial strength.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. The company is fundamentally an oil producer and seller with a focus on exporting that oil. The company as a result is dependent on Brent prices for cash flow, prices that are currently weak as tariffs and markets make demand uncertain. That lack of cash flow fundamentally hurts the company's ability to drive FCF.

The second piece of this is the uncertainty with the Brazilian government. The company has faced massive corruption scandals with the government and in many ways, its revenue is used as a way to benefit Brazil versus the company's shareholder returns. Continued government risk could hurt the company's ability to drive returns.

Conclusion

Petrobras has an impressive portfolio of assets. The company is actively revamping its portfolio and is bringing online new FPSOs supporting increasing production as it invests massively within its business. That has led to substantial lease debt for the company, but it's debts that the company can comfortably afford.

The company does have risks as a result of oil prices along with governance risks with the Brazilian government's control, however, overall, we expect the company to be able to continue driving substantial shareholder returns. That makes the company a valuable investment opportunity. Please let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Buying PepsiCo With A Rising Dividend At A Discount
data.description:
Introduction

My investing guidelines and portfolio focus are on investing for income that provides ample yield, safety, and annual increases that are above my expected long-term rate of inflation (3%). I want rising dividends because I want rising income during my retirement. I also require the stocks I invest in to have demonstrated a competitive advantage that has proven unassailable for many years. That proof comes in the form of stable and predictable financial results even through periods of market turmoil like we had in 2001-03 and 2007-09. PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) always survives and is able to thrive in the aftermath.

In addition to those requirements, I look for stocks of companies that are trading at what I have determined as a discount to fair value. I want a compound annual return of at least 10%. I do not plan on selling for at least five years. I have been retired since 2002 and continue to build additional layers of income each year through dividends. Layer upon layer of income that increases every year is my formula for a worry-free retirement.

I want to begin by pointing out that the equity universe is divided today into two groups: The New World Economy (Mega Cap Growth) and the Old World Economy.

New World Economy

The New World Economy is made up primarily of mega cap technology companies, along with some peripheral businesses that benefit the most from enabling the New World Economy, such as large financial institutions and suppliers.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DIA), NASDAQ 100 (QQQ), and the S&P 500 Index (SPY) are dominated by these behemoths due to weighting, especially QQQ and SPY. Both indexes are weighted by market capitalization, which means that the largest companies can dominate the index movements. The largest companies, such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta (META), and Tesla (TSLA), range in market cap from $1 trillion to $3 trillion. Smaller companies that are not involved in AI (Artificial Intelligence) or EVs (Electric Vehicles) cannot influence the index with relatively small capitalizations of $10 billion to $300 billion.

Old World Economy

Those small companies make up the vast majority of the economy and that is why I call them the Old World Economy. Companies like PepsiCo, with a market capitalization of a mere $180 billion, are in the S&P 500 but have little influence over how the index moves.

When passive investors buy shares of SPY or QQQ, most of those dollars end up in the mega cap companies’ shares because of the weighting. When investors buy shares in one or more of the mega cap companies, the entire index is lifted. Since the advent of AI investing, very little money is flowing into shares of those companies that represent the majority of the economy.

It is like the shares of much of our economy got left behind even though the “market” is flirting with making new highs.

Why PepsiCo shares have fallen

There is a lot of chatter about how the weight-loss drugs, GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic and Wegovy (among others), are reducing demand for sodas and snacks, the core of PepsiCo’s business. But I have another take on why demand has stagnated.

As pointed out in a very well-written analysis by Eugenio Catone, aggressive price hikes have forced consumers to think twice about buying that bag of potato chips. Price increases of 14% in 2022 and 13% in 2023 of some of its popular product lines caused a decrease in unit demand.

But there is more to the price drop than stagnant demand. Also, from the same article linked above, the cost of capital is weighing on PepsiCo because the company has taken on debt to facilitate acquisitions in recent years at a rate that is much higher than is customary. This is temporary for two reasons: First, borrowing costs will eventually come down over the next two to three years as the Fed cut interest rates. Second, PepsiCo will adjust its capital allocation over the next few years to include less funding committed to acquisitions.

The perception of the investing public is driving the price down with worry about the company’s ability to maintain its dividend. This will become a non-issue due to steps I expect the company to take as described in the previous paragraph which will allow the company to generate more free cash flow. That, in turn, will enable PepsiCo to continue it streak of raising its dividend annually for 52 consecutive years. Over the last five years, the company has raised its dividend by an average of 7.25% per year. I expect that rate of increase will slow to 6% per year going forward as the company finds it somewhat more difficult to achieve the levels of earnings growth necessary to support the higher rate of increase.

I believe that there is concern over the long-term impact of GLP-1 drugs reducing the craving for snacks and sodas. I’m not buying it! Many (not all) of the people who are using weight loss drugs will go right back to their old eating habits as soon as they go off the meds. They are losing weight so they can enjoy the life they want to live and that life will include snacks and sodas.

The world population continues to grow and PepsiCo, while already operating in 200 countries has more penetration to accomplish in many of the emerging markets around the world before it hits saturation. Unit growth will return and profits will climb.

Calculating the Expected Total Return

Mr. Catone estimated that the shares are 15-20% undervalued. The shares have fallen by almost 24% over the last year and by 33.8% from the all-time high achieved in May 2023.

Looking back at history, PepsiCo’s average P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio over the last ten years is about 22.4, while it is currently trading at 16.5 times earnings. This means that as growth in revenue and profits return to normal levels, so should the multiple, which equates to a discount of about 35%. This assumes that interest rates fall to more favorable levels to reduce the company’s cost of capital and interest expense in the future. I do not expect the shares to rebound overnight, but do believe there will be a reversion to the mean effect over the next five years.

If we assume the multiple expansion is achieved by May 2030, we can assume that PepsiCo’s share price will rise by an average of 6.3% per year to accommodate a reversion to the mean in valuation.

(((22.4/16.5)^(1/5))-1)= 6.3%

Add to that the current dividend yield of 4.3% along with an expected growth in earnings of 6% per year and we end up with an expected total annual return of 16.7% over the next five years.

Risks

The most obvious risk is that I could be wrong about consumer habits changing due to GLP-1 drugs. If that is the case, and people who lose weight using these drugs stay away from (or dramatically reduce intake of) sodas and salty snacks permanently, there is a risk that growth may never recover.

Another potential risk is that management does not slow down on acquisitions and continues to pile up debt to unsustainable levels. This would reduce the company's long-term capital allocation flexibility and increase interest expense. The result would be that the company could no longer continue to increase the dividend annually as it has done for 52 years.

While this last risk is possible, I do not think that management will risk breaking the company's record of rising dividends. Although such things have happened in other companies, and it could happen to PepsiCo, my sincere belief is that management will do whatever it takes to maintain that streak.

That brings us to a third potential risk. If the company continues to use debt to acquire brands with future growth, it may also need to use debt to fund the dividends. Such a model is not sustainable over the long term and could prevent the company from maintaining its stellar credit rating, thereby increasing its cost of capital. Again, I believe management at PepsiCo is cognizant of these risks and will avoid making such mistakes. However, it behooves us, as investors, to consider all the possibilities.

Conclusion

Many companies like PepsiCo have been left behind in the Old World Economy valuation scheme that is predominant today. I believe that paradigm will be reevaluated over the next 3-5 years to the benefit of steady, predictable growth and rising dividends. The volatility of the New World Economy stocks will eventually unnerve enough investors and drive them back to the basics of fundamental investing. That will favor dividend champions like PepsiCo.

I believe the dividend is safe and will continue to grow at about the rate of future earnings growth (6% per year). Locking in a potential 16.7% compound annual return on an investment today is a good choice. I rate PepsiCo stock a buy.

data.title: Cadence Bank: Shares Get Cheaper Amid Mounting Economic Uncertainty
data.description:
It has been around eight months since I first covered Southern lender Cadence Bank (NYSE:CADE). Looking back, my bullish case for Cadence really rested on its lending margin potential, as this bank simply stood to gain more from balance sheet repricing dynamics than peers did. That has largely played, albeit to no effect on the stock, with these shares actually performing a little worse than the broader regional bank space in that time.

While that period has seen plenty of economic turbulence - with the market still weighing up the impact of the current administration's trade policies - I'd note that consensus earnings estimates aren't actually any lower for Cadence than they were back then. Given that maps to some relatively cheap-looking valuation multiples, I remain bullish.

NIM Expansion Plays Out, But Pre-Provision Growth Has Been Weak

After lagging when rates were rising, I thought lower interest rates would help Cadence more than the industry. Put simply, while Cadence's funding costs had risen sharply, it had simultaneously seen a relatively modest pass-through rate on its assets during the hiking cycle, squeezing its net interest margin.

As expected, NIM trends have been good since my opening piece. On the liability side, the Fed's rate cuts have driven around 40bps of improvement in Cadence's interest costs (expressed relative to total interest-earning assets). On the asset side, Cadence's yield has fallen by a much more modest 19bps, aided by fixed-rate assets repricing upwards. This has resulted in roughly 20bps of total NIM expansion, which is a better performance than the banking system as a whole (up around 9bps in that time to 3.25%).

Unfortunately, balance sheet growth appears to have been a weak spot for Cadence. Interest-earning assets have fallen around 3% since my opening piece to $43.6 billion, with Cadence underperforming the wider industry here. As a result, margin improvement has only led to relatively modest growth in quarterly net interest revenue, which has risen by less than 2% to around $363 million.

This has driven a disappointing performance on the pre-provision line. Whereas the industry has seen roughly mid-single-digit growth in pretax pre-provision profit in that time, Cadence's has been virtually flat after adjusting for one-off items.

Positively, management estimates that both deposits and loans will grow low-to-mid-single-digits this year, and that revenue growth will outpace cost growth, implying a return to pre-provision profit growth.

Shares Flat As Attention Turns To Tariffs

Per the introduction, Cadence's stock has ended up about flat since my last piece, albeit with large movements on either side. For all the recent turbulence, I would note Cadence's bottom line outlook remains largely unchanged. Consensus 2025 EPS ($2.89) is around 3% higher than it was back then, and while 2026 consensus has seen a larger jump (to $3.41), this is partly due to deal accretion relating to Cadence's upcoming acquisition of Industry Bankshares. Excluding this, standalone 2026 EPS projections also haven't moved much (consensus was around $3.10-$3.15 per share before the deal announcement).

When I opened on it, Cadence was trading for around 12x then-forward EPS. Given the current $30.23 quote, and assuming 2025 plays out as the sell-side expects it to, Cadence's current forward P/E is only around 10.5x. That falls to less than 9x based on the 2026 consensus. Similarly, Cadence has delivered 11% growth in tangible book value per share since my opening piece (to $22.30). Since the share price has been flat, its P/TBVPS multiple has contracted by around 15bps to 1.35x.

Looking back, both of those metrics are now at double-digit discounts to their recent historical averages. Cadence's trailing 12-month dividend yield is also around 30% above its ten-year average, as shown below.

Granted, attention has increasingly turned to the possible negative impact of U.S. trade policy on the economy, but I don't think credit risk represents an outsized issue for Cadence. Cadence's net charge-offs have been flat in recent quarters. At 27bps, they are also roughly in line with the peer average. Ditto for its non-performing loans ratio, which has now registered two sequential periods of improvement.

It is true that Cadence does more lending than the average bank, as loans constitute just over 70% of its assets compared to a circa 65% average for peers. In a downturn, names with less loan concentration may be a safer bet.

More important than this is that, one, Cadence has an above-average NIM, and two, at over 1.6% in 1Q25, it also earns an above-average pre-provision return on its assets. Put simply, while Cadence does more lending than the average bank, it also has a greater ability to absorb credit charges.

Cadence's capital cushion also appears to be healthy. There will be some dilution from the Industry deal due to fair value accounting, but a projected post-close CET1 ratio of 11.0% remains solid.

Looking back, Cadence has grown earnings per share by 115% over the past decade (equal to roughly 8% annualized). Adjusted for the unrealized losses on its bond portfolio, tangible book value per share has risen by around 80% in that time (equal to just over 6% annualized).

Cadence's current quarterly dividend is $0.275 per share, for a conservative payout ratio of roughly 40% of net income. Cadence has plenty of room to fund strong earnings and balance sheet growth ahead, including inorganically via share buybacks and more M&A.

A sub-9x P/E on the 2026 consensus looks much too cheap for this. If Cadence simply traded on its current 10.5x forward EPS multiple a year from now, the stock could offer ~18%-19% upside, rising to ~22% including the dividend.

Conclusion

Cadence Bank has been a somewhat disappointing performer since I first covered it last September. Net interest margin expansion has largely played out as I had hoped but, after a lot of volatility, the shares have remained virtually flat. Taking the positive from that, this performance means that Cadence has become even cheaper than it was back then, with its P/E ratio, P/TBVPS multiple, and dividend yield all looking attractive based on their past averages. While tariff turmoil could hit banks in the near term, I don't have any particular concerns with Cadence from a credit perspective. Now looks like a good entry point, and I remain bullish.

data.title: Rebuilt And Re-Rated: Woodward Belongs In The Quality Growth Bucket
data.description:
Woodward (NASDAQ:WWD) is no longer just a cyclical industrial parts supplier—it's evolving into a precision systems platform company with strong exposure to structurally growing markets like defense, aerospace aftermarket, and energy resilience. This strategic shift is starting to be recognized by the market, as reflected in the recent breakout of the stock. Investors are increasingly buying into the idea that Woodward isn’t just rebounding—it’s building a sustained, high-margin growth trajectory.

At the core of the investment thesis is Woodward’s aerospace segment, which now accounts for over 60% of total revenue and remains its most profitable business line. Within aerospace, defense OEM is showing exceptional momentum—up 52% in Q2—driven by demand from long-cycle Smart Defense programs like DDG-51, JDAM, and KDDX. These programs come with deep backlogs and contracts that extend beyond 2025, offering visibility and consistency.

Backing all of this is a structurally improving margin profile. Aerospace margins hit a record 22.2% in Q2, thanks to scale, lean manufacturing, and smarter pricing. The company is now guiding to normalized incremental margins of 30–35% in the second half—solid numbers that suggest profitability is sustainable, not propped up by short-term gains.

For investors, the story here is compelling. Woodward is transitioning into a margin-accretive growth name—a rare setup in the mid-cap industrial space. With demand tailwinds in place, pricing power holding, and execution solid, there’s still meaningful upside as volumes normalize and the market continues to re-rate the stock accordingly. Let’s take a deeper look and break it down.

Top-Line Growth Drivers

Aerospace is clearly picking up speed again, and it’s happening across all segments. In the commercial aftermarket, Q2 saw a 23% jump year-over-year, thanks to strong demand for maintenance and high aircraft utilization. That momentum isn’t expected to fade anytime soon—management’s still guiding for high single-digit growth in the second half, which puts Woodward ahead of most players in the space.

On the defense side, OEM sales surged 52% from a year ago, powered by demand from Smart Defense initiatives and strength in propulsion systems tied to key platforms like the DDG-51 and KDDX. These programs run long and are riding geopolitical tailwinds, giving the segment strong visibility. Meanwhile, the commercial OE side has hit a temporary speed bump due to ongoing issues at Boeing. Still, with 787 production ramping back up—particularly around GE’s GEnx engines—there’s good reason to believe activity will pick up again soon, which bodes well for Woodward’s exposure through GE.

Zooming out, aerospace now makes up around 64% of total revenue and stands as Woodward’s most profitable and durable growth engine. It gives the company a solid foothold in long-term themes like the post-pandemic recovery in air travel, rising global defense budgets, and increasing production of next-gen platforms.

As for the industrial side of the business, it’s holding up well in spite of a 5% overall decline. When you exclude China, core industrial sales were actually up 11% year-over-year. Oil and gas led the way with 21% growth, tied to infrastructure expansion, LNG projects, and broader power generation demand. Marine transportation climbed 13%, benefiting from a shipbuilding boom expected to last into the next decade. Power generation posted a modest 4% gain, lifted by growing needs from data centers and grid resilience investments. This side of the business helps cushion the ups and downs of aerospace and keeps earnings steadier. With exposure to China now limited and under control, the rest of the industrial portfolio is showing healthy global demand tied to energy and infrastructure trends.

Margin Expansion Drivers

Margins, especially in aerospace, are in a good place. Segment margin in Q2 hit 22.2%, a company record and up 240 basis points from last year. That kind of gain didn’t come from just one lever—it was a mix of stronger pricing, a favorable aftermarket skew, and disciplined cost management through lean manufacturing. For the second half, Woodward’s expecting incrementals of 30–35%. That’s below the 40% hit in Q2 but still solid, and a sign that margins are moving in the right direction for the long haul. Even with more volume coming from lower-margin OE and defense work, the structural economics still look strong.

Pricing is also playing a bigger role. In Q2, it contributed around 7% to total growth, with aerospace again leading the charge. Woodward’s getting better at charging what its platforms are worth—especially in mission-critical areas where performance and reliability matter most. The company’s value-based pricing toolkit seems to be working, and investors tend to reward firms that can turn technical strength into margin durability.

Operationally, things continue to improve. Facilities like Rockford and Zealand are seeing the benefits of production upgrades, smoother onboarding, and greater throughput—all while keeping costs down. The regional supply chain strategy also helps by reducing the impact of tariffs and currency swings. These gains aren’t just cyclical boosts—they’re baked into how the company runs, and the margin benefits should keep building over time.

By the Numbers

Q2 FY25 came in strong, with revenue at $884 million—up 6% from the year before. Aerospace was up 13%, and core industrial climbed 11%. Adjusted earnings per share landed at $1.69, or $1.78 on a GAAP basis, compared to $1.62 last year. Free cash flow hit $60 million for the first half, a solid number considering the working capital investment. Aerospace margins reached an all-time high of 22.2%, and the company bumped up the low end of its full-year EPS guidance to $5.95–$6.25, reflecting confidence in how the rest of the year will play out.

What’s Getting Investors Excited
